title: Fix(graph): Allow any Line subclass as edge_type in Graph/DiGraph
body:
## Overview: What does this pull request change?
<!--changelog-start-->
Fix #4250 
<!--changelog-end-->
**More broadly, this fix allows any subclass of `Line` to be used as an `edge_type`** in the `Graph` and `DiGraph`, making the implementation more flexible and extensible.

## Motivation and Explanation: Why and how do your changes improve the library?
Previously, the `Graph` and `DiGraph` classes assumed that the `edge_type`  will always have its first two arguments as start and end point.
But, such is not true for subclasses of `Line`. This change lets user use any subclass of `Line` in `Graph` and `DiGraph`.

## Changes

- **Refactored Graph and DiGraph:**
  - Fixed edge handling logic to support all subclasses of `Line`, not just `LabeledLine`.
  - Modified `_add_edge` accordingly.
- **Testing:**
  - Added/updated tests for `LabeledLine` as edge types in `test_graph.py`.

step_plan.summary: Update Graph and DiGraph edge creation to construct edges using keyword arguments (start=, end=) so any Line subclass—including those with non-standard __init__ positional parameters like LabeledLine—can be used as edge_type. Add/adjust graph tests to cover both construction-time and add_edges-time usage of LabeledLine as an edge type.

steps.operation: Refactor edge instantiation in _add_edge to use keyword start/end and safely merge edge_config overrides
steps.reason: Current implementation assumes the first two positional args of edge_type are (start, end), which breaks for Line subclasses like LabeledLine that take other positional args (e.g. label) and require start/end as keyword arguments. Centralizing a robust construction strategy here makes add_edges/add_edge consistent and backwards-compatible.
steps.side_effects: May change which values win when both edge_config and internal logic specify start/end; ensure explicit start/end derived from vertices cannot be accidentally overridden (or document/guard). Could affect any downstream code relying on positional construction or on edge_config overriding endpoints; run existing graph tests and edge-related scenes for regressions.

steps.operation: Align initial edge population with new construction semantics (use start=/end= keywords when creating each edge)
steps.reason: Graph’s initial edge creation during __init__ must not use positional start/end; otherwise Graph(edge_type=LabeledLine, edge_config={"label": ...}) still fails at construction time even if _add_edge is fixed for later additions.
steps.side_effects: Could alter how per-edge configs are applied at construction vs. later add_edges calls; verify edge z_index/buff/path_arc defaults remain unchanged and that edge dict keys and stored objects remain compatible with existing APIs.

steps.operation: Make directed edge population use the same keyword-based start/end construction and preserve any tip/arrow-specific behavior
steps.reason: DiGraph is subject to the same constructor-signature issue; it must also be able to build edges using any Line subclass. Additionally, DiGraph may apply directed-edge specifics (tips, arrow styles), which must remain functional after refactor.
steps.side_effects: Potential interaction with arrow/tip configuration if DiGraph previously depended on a specific edge_type like Arrow; verify Arrow and other common directed edge types still render correctly, and confirm no double-tip or missing-tip regressions.

steps.operation: Audit/update edge updater logic to be signature-agnostic and rely only on Line APIs (set_points_by_ends) and stable config access
steps.reason: Even if edges can be constructed, they must continue to update correctly when vertices move. The updater should not assume constructor arg ordering; it should update geometry via set_points_by_ends (supported by Line subclasses) and handle any needed parameters (buff/path_arc) without depending on edge_type init signature.
steps.side_effects: If updater currently reads parameters from edge_config in a way that is inconsistent across edge types, this could change runtime behavior (e.g., buff handling). Validate with existing tests and any scenes that animate vertex movement; ensure labels (LabeledLine) stay attached and edges don’t lose styling on update.

steps.operation: Ensure directed edge updater keeps endpoints and tips consistent for arbitrary Line subclasses
steps.reason: DiGraph updating must remain correct when using non-standard Line subclasses as edges. The updater should update endpoints via set_points_by_ends and preserve directed-edge affordances (tips) without relying on init-time positional args.
steps.side_effects: Tip placement or orientation might change if update logic is sensitive to buff/path_arc; confirm Arrow-like edges still behave and that LabeledLine (if used in DiGraph) doesn’t break updater assumptions. Run directed-graph related tests (if present) and consider adding a smoke test if gaps remain.

steps.operation: Extend add_edges test coverage to include a Line subclass with non-(start,end) positional signature (e.g., LabeledLine) and required edge_config (label)
steps.reason: This is the regression surface described in the PR: add_edges should succeed with edge_type=LabeledLine when label is provided via edge_config, proving that edge construction no longer depends on positional start/end.
steps.side_effects: May require importing LabeledLine and adjusting assertions to avoid brittle rendering/geometry checks; keep it to type/instance and edge-count assertions. Could expose any remaining mismatches between Graph construction and add_edges behavior.

steps.operation: Add a construction-time smoke test verifying Graph can be instantiated with edge_type=LabeledLine and produces correct edge objects
steps.reason: Covers the other main entry point: Graph(..., edges=..., edge_type=...) should work at initialization, not only when edges are added after construction. Ensures _populate_edge_dict uses the same robust construction approach.
steps.side_effects: Test may need to avoid relying on exact label positioning or LaTeX availability; prefer a simple label (e.g., Text/MarkupText if supported by LabeledLine expectations) or a minimal label object accepted by LabeledLine. Running this test might require additional test dependencies if label creation is non-trivial.
